title: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction
authors: David A. Morrow, Elliott M. Antman, Joseph Loscalzo

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis in AMI Management
items.content: Early diagnosis and management are crucial for patients with ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) due to high mortality rates, especially before hospital admission. The 12-lead ECG is vital for triaging STEMI from other acute coronary syndromes.
keyPoints: Approximately 605,000 new AMIs and 200,000 recurrent AMIs occur annually in the U.S.
In-hospital mortality rate has declined to ~5%
1-year mortality after AMI is ~15%
Mortality is fourfold higher in patients aged >75 years
The ECG distinguishes STEMI from other acute coronary syndromes
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction

items.title: Role of Cardiac Biomarkers and ECG in AMI Diagnosis
items.content: Cardiac biomarkers, alongside the 12-lead ECG, are essential for differentiating between unstable angina (UA), non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and STEMI. They help estimate the extent of myocardial necrosis in STEMI cases.
keyPoints: ECG is central to management decisions
Cardiac biomarkers distinguish UA from NSTEMI and assess myocardial injury in STEMI
Epidemiologic shift: more patients with NSTEMI than STEMI
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: ROLE OF ACUTE PLAQUE RUPTURE/EROSION
items.title: Mechanism of STEMI due to Acute Plaque Rupture/Erosion
items.content: STEMI typically results from a thrombotic occlusion following acute plaque rupture or erosion, exposing its contents and promoting thrombogenesis. This process involves platelet activation and aggregation facilitated by various agonists.
keyPoints: STEMI occurs when an atherosclerotic plaque is disrupted, leading to rapid coronary artery thrombus formation.
Histologic studies show that plaques with a rich lipid core and thin fibrous cap are prone to disruption.
Less than 5% of such thin-capped fibroatheromas lead to AMI during long-term follow-up.
Platelet activation involves the release of thromboxane A2, promoting vasoconstriction and resistance to fibrinolysis.
Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor changes allow platelets to bind fibrinogen, leading to aggregation.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: ROLE OF ACUTE PLAQUE RUPTURE/EROSION

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: ROLE OF ACUTE PLAQUE RUPTURE/EROSION
items.title: Coagulation Cascade Activation in STEMI
items.content: The coagulation cascade is triggered by tissue factor exposure at the site of plaque disruption, leading to thrombin generation and fibrin formation, which occludes the coronary artery.
keyPoints: Tissue factor exposure activates factors VII and X, initiating the coagulation cascade.
Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin, forming a thrombus with platelet aggregates.
Fluid-phase and clot-bound thrombin amplify the coagulation process.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: ROLE OF ACUTE PLAQUE RUPTURE/EROSION

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: ROLE OF ACUTE PLAQUE RUPTURE/EROSION
items.title: Factors Influencing Myocardial Damage in STEMI
items.content: The extent of myocardial damage from coronary occlusion depends on several factors, including the affected vessel's territory, occlusion duration, and collateral blood supply.
keyPoints: Myocardial damage is influenced by the territory supplied by the occluded vessel.
Total occlusion and duration of occlusion are critical determinants of damage extent.
Collateral circulation can mitigate damage by supplying blood to affected tissue.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: ROLE OF ACUTE PLAQUE RUPTURE/EROSION

items.title: Recognizing STEMI Presentation and Differentiating from Other Conditions
items.content: STEMI often presents with severe, persistent chest pain that can mimic other conditions. Key distinguishing features include the nature of the pain (heavy, squeezing, or crushing) and its duration, which is typically longer than angina pectoris. Pain may radiate to various areas but not below the umbilicus. The presence of sweating, nausea, vomiting, anxiety, and a sense of impending doom are common accompaniments.
keyPoints: Pain in STEMI is deep, visceral, described as heavy, squeezing, or crushing.
STEMI pain may radiate to arms, abdomen, back, lower jaw, neck, but not below the umbilicus.
Accompanied by sweating, nausea, vomiting, anxiety, and a sense of impending doom.
Painless STEMI is more common in patients with diabetes mellitus and increases with age.
Differential diagnosis includes acute pericarditis (radiation to trapezius), pulmonary embolism, acute aortic dissection, costochondritis, gastrointestinal disorders.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Understanding Precipitating Factors and Atypical Presentations of STEMI
items.content: STEMI may be preceded by factors such as vigorous physical exercise, emotional stress, or illness. It can occur at any time but often clusters in the morning hours after awakening. Atypical presentations include sudden-onset breathlessness, loss of consciousness, confusional state, profound weakness, arrhythmia onset, peripheral embolism, or unexplained arterial pressure drop.
keyPoints: Precipitating factors for STEMI can include vigorous exercise, emotional stress, or illness.
STEMI clusters are often seen in the morning within a few hours of awakening.
Atypical presentations may involve sudden breathlessness, loss of consciousness, confusional state, profound weakness, arrhythmia, peripheral embolism, or unexplained arterial pressure drop.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Recognizing STEMI through Physical Findings
items.content: Patients with ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) often exhibit specific physical signs that can guide diagnosis. These include restlessness, pallor, diaphoresis, and substernal chest pain lasting more than 30 minutes. The presence of sympathetic or parasympathetic nervous system manifestations depends on the infarct location: anterior infarctions may present with tachycardia/hypertension while inferior ones may show bradycardia/hypotension.
keyPoints: Substernal chest pain >30 min and diaphoresis strongly suggest STEMI
Anterior infarction: sympathetic hyperactivity (tachycardia, hypertension)
Inferior infarction: parasympathetic hyperactivity (bradycardia, hypotension)
Physical signs of ventricular dysfunction include fourth and third heart sounds, decreased first heart sound intensity, paradoxical splitting of the second heart sound
Transient midsystolic or late systolic apical murmur due to mitral valve dysfunction may be present
Pericardial friction rub may occur with transmural STEMI
Carotid pulse often decreased in volume reflecting reduced stroke volume
Temperature elevations up to 38°C may be observed during the first week after STEMI
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PHYSICAL FINDINGS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.title: Understanding STEMI Progression through Laboratory Findings
items.content: STEMI progresses in distinct temporal stages: acute (first few hours to 7 days), healing (7–28 days), and healed (≥29 days). During these phases, the myocardium undergoes cellular responses starting with polymorphonuclear leukocytes for debris clearance, followed by monocytes. The sequence involves initial proinflammatory cytokines from repair monocytes leading to angiogenesis and extracellular matrix production, crucial for granulation tissue formation and scar development.
keyPoints: STEMI progresses through acute (first few hours–7 days), healing (7–28 days), healed (≥29 days) stages
Initial recruitment of polymorphonuclear leukocytes followed by monocytes in the infarct zone
Proinflammatory cytokines and repair monocytes stimulate angiogenesis and extracellular matrix production
Formation of granulation tissue with new microvessels and fibrosis is key for myocardial scar formation
Role of emergency hematopoiesis in mobilizing leukocyte progenitor cells for myocardial healing
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PHYSICAL FINDINGS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | CARDIAC BIOMARKERS OF MYOCARDIAL INJURY
items.title: Understanding ECG Changes in STEMI and Their Implications
items.content: ST-segment elevation indicates total occlusion of an epicardial coronary artery, often leading to Q waves on the ECG. However, not all patients with initial ST-segment elevation develop Q waves due to factors like transient obstruction or a rich collateral network.
keyPoints: ST-segment elevation suggests total occlusion of an epicardial coronary artery.
Most patients with initial ST-segment elevation evolve into Q waves on the ECG.
Q waves may not appear if the thrombus is not totally occlusive or a rich collateral network is present.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | CARDIAC BIOMARKERS OF MYOCARDIAL INJURY

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | CARDIAC BIOMARKERS OF MYOCARDIAL INJURY
items.title: Role of Cardiac Biomarkers in Diagnosing Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Cardiac biomarkers, particularly cardiac-specific troponins (cTnT and cTnI), are crucial for diagnosing myocardial infarction. They become detectable when their levels rise above the 99th percentile of the upper reference limit.
keyPoints: Cardiac-specific troponin T (cTnT) and I (cTnI) are preferred biochemical markers for MI.
Biomarker values must show a rise and/or fall with at least one value above the 99th percentile of the upper reference limit.
High-sensitivity assays for cTnT or cTnI remain elevated for 7–10 days post-STEMI.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | CARDIAC BIOMARKERS OF MYOCARDIAL INJURY

items.subtopic: ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | CARDIAC BIOMARKERS OF MYOCARDIAL INJURY
items.title: Differentiating STEMI from NSTEMI Using ECG and Biomarkers
items.content: STEMI is characterized by ST-segment elevation, while NSTEMI lacks this feature but shows elevated cardiac biomarkers. The presence of Q waves or transient ST/T changes does not perfectly correlate with the transmurality of infarction.
keyPoints: STEMI involves ST-segment elevation; NSTEMI does not.
Q-wave development depends more on infarct volume than transmurality.
ECG-pathologic correlations are imperfect, leading to reliance on biomarkers for diagnosis.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ELECTROCARDIOGRAM | CARDIAC BIOMARKERS OF MYOCARDIAL INJURY

items.title: Role of Echocardiography in STEMI Management
items.content: Echocardiography is crucial for detecting wall motion abnormalities, which are almost universally present in STEMI patients. It aids management decisions by identifying the presence or absence of these abnormalities when ECGs are non-diagnostic.
keyPoints: Wall motion abnormalities detected by echocardiography aid in deciding reperfusion therapy (e.g., PCI or fibrinolysis).
Echocardiographic estimation of LV function is prognostically useful and indicates the need for renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors.
Doppler echocardiography detects serious complications like ventricular septal defect and mitral regurgitation.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CARDIAC IMAGING | PREHOSPITAL CARE

items.title: Limitations of Radionuclide Imaging in STEMI
items.content: Radionuclide imaging, while available, is rarely used for STEMI evaluation due to its inability to distinguish between acute infarcts and chronic scars.
keyPoints: Myocardial perfusion imaging with $[^{99m}\mathrm{Tc}]$-sestamibi reveals defects but cannot differentiate acute from chronic myocardial damage.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CARDIAC IMAGING | PREHOSPITAL CARE

items.title: Importance of Prompt Prehospital Care in STEMI
items.content: Prehospital care focuses on reducing delays between symptom onset and treatment initiation, as most out-of-hospital deaths from STEMI occur due to ventricular fibrillation within the first 24 hours.
keyPoints: Most out-of-hospital deaths from STEMI result from ventricular fibrillation, with over half occurring in the first hour of symptoms.
Key elements include rapid recognition and response to symptoms, defibrillation capability, and expedited reperfusion therapy.
Educating the public on recognizing chest discomfort can reduce delays in seeking medical attention.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CARDIAC IMAGING | PREHOSPITAL CARE

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT IN THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT | TABLE 286-1 Definitions of Myocardial Injury and Infarction Criteria for Myocardial Injury | Criteria for Acute Myocardial Infarction (types 1, 2, and 3 MI)
items.title: Optimizing STEMI Management in the Emergency Department
items.content: In managing suspected ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) in the emergency department, prioritize rapid identification of patients for urgent reperfusion therapy. Initiate treatment promptly to minimize time from first medical contact to reperfusion initiation. Ensure appropriate triage and avoid discharging STEMI patients prematurely.
keyPoints: Goals include control of cardiac discomfort, rapid identification for reperfusion therapy, proper patient triage, and avoiding inappropriate discharge.
Minimize the time from first medical contact to initiation of reperfusion therapy.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · MANAGEMENT IN THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT | TABLE 286-1 Definitions of Myocardial Injury and Infarction Criteria for Myocardial Injury | Criteria for Acute Myocardial Infarction (types 1, 2, and 3 MI)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT IN THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT | TABLE 286-1 Definitions of Myocardial Injury and Infarction Criteria for Myocardial Injury | Criteria for Acute Myocardial Infarction (types 1, 2, and 3 MI)
items.title: Criteria for Diagnosing Acute Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Acute myocardial infarction is diagnosed when there's evidence of acute myocardial injury with clinical signs of ischemia. Key diagnostic criteria include elevated cardiac troponin (cTn) levels above the 99th percentile URL, accompanied by symptoms or ECG changes indicative of ischemia, new pathologic Q waves, imaging showing loss of viable myocardium, or identification of a coronary thrombus.
keyPoints: Acute MI diagnosis requires elevated cTn levels above the 99th percentile URL with clinical evidence of ischemia.

Criteria include symptoms of ischemia, new ischemic ECG changes, pathologic Q waves, imaging evidence of myocardial loss or wall motion abnormality, and coronary thrombus identification (except for types 2 and 3 MIs).

Type 1 MI involves postmortem atherothrombosis; type 2 MI results from an imbalance in oxygen supply/demand unrelated to atherothrombosis; type 3 MI is cardiac death with presumed ischemic ECG changes before cTn testing.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · MANAGEMENT IN THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT | TABLE 286-1 Definitions of Myocardial Injury and Infarction Criteria for Myocardial Injury | Criteria for Acute Myocardial Infarction (types 1, 2, and 3 MI)

items.subtopic: Criteria for Coronary Procedure-Related MI (types 4 and 5 MI)
items.title: Understanding Criteria for PCI and CABG-related MI
items.content: Coronary procedure-related myocardial infarction is categorized into type 4a for PCI-related events and type 5 for CABG-related events. The criteria involve specific elevations in cardiac troponin (cTn) levels post-procedure, with thresholds set at >5 times the 99th percentile upper reference limit for type 4a MI and >10 times for type 5 MI within 48 hours of the procedure.
keyPoints: Type 4 MI is PCI-related; Type 5 MI is CABG-related.
cTn elevation criteria: >5x 99th percentile URL for type 4a MI, >10x for type 5 MI within 48h post-procedure.
Preprocedural cTn levels stable or falling must show a >20% change from baseline and meet the fold increase criteria.
New ischemic ECG changes, new pathologic Q waves, imaging evidence of loss of viable myocardium, angiographic findings consistent with procedural complications are required for diagnosis.
Type 4B MI (stent thrombosis) and Type 4C MI (restenosis) meet type 1 MI criteria.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · Criteria for Coronary Procedure-Related MI (types 4 and 5 MI) | Criteria for Prior or Silent/Unrecognized MI

items.subtopic: Criteria for Prior or Silent/Unrecognized MI
items.title: Diagnosing Prior or Silent Myocardial Infarction
items.content: A diagnosis of prior or silent myocardial infarction can be established through specific clinical and imaging criteria. These include the presence of abnormal Q waves, imaging evidence of loss of viable myocardium consistent with ischemic etiology, or pathoanatomical findings indicative of a past MI.
keyPoints: Abnormal Q waves with/without symptoms in absence of nonischemic causes indicate prior MI.
Imaging showing loss of viable myocardium in an ischemic pattern confirms prior MI.
Pathoanatomical evidence of PCI-capable prior MI necessitates prompt intervention.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · Criteria for Coronary Procedure-Related MI (types 4 and 5 MI) | Criteria for Prior or Silent/Unrecognized MI

items.title: Use of Nitroglycerin in STEMI for Pain and Ischemia Control
items.content: Sublingual nitroglycerin is recommended for most patients with STEMI to alleviate chest discomfort. It works by decreasing myocardial oxygen demand and increasing supply, thus addressing ischemic symptoms.
keyPoints: Up to three doses of 0.4 mg at 5-min intervals can be given.
Avoid in systolic pressure <90 mmHg or suspected RV infarction.
Do not administer within 24 hours after phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor use.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CONTROL OF DISCOMFORT

items.title: Morphine Administration for STEMI Pain Relief
items.content: Morphine is effective in relieving pain associated with STEMI, though it may cause hemodynamic changes such as reduced cardiac output and arterial pressure.
keyPoints: Administer 2–4 mg intravenously every 5 minutes.
Monitor for side effects like bradycardia or nausea; manage with atropine if needed.
Does not significantly affect antiplatelet therapy outcomes.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CONTROL OF DISCOMFORT

items.title: Intravenous Beta Blockers in STEMI Management
items.content: Beta blockers like metoprolol can reduce ischemic pain and lower the risk of reinfarction and ventricular fibrillation by decreasing myocardial oxygen demand.
keyPoints: Metoprolol regimen: 5 mg every 2–5 min for three doses, with heart rate >60 bpm and systolic pressure >100 mmHg.
Initiate oral therapy after intravenous dose if no contraindications are present.
Avoid in patients with signs of acute heart failure or other specific contraindications.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · CONTROL OF DISCOMFORT

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | LIMITATION OF INFARCT SIZE | TABLE 286-2 Electrocardiographic Criteria for ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction
items.title: Criteria and Importance of Reperfusion Therapy in STEMI
items.content: Reperfusion therapy is critical when a patient presents with ST-segment elevation meeting the Universal Definition of MI criteria. This includes deciding between fibrinolysis and primary PCI, as fibrinolysis is not beneficial without ST-elevation and may be harmful.
keyPoints: Initial 12-lead ECG is essential for triage decisions in STEMI.
Reperfusion therapy should be considered if ST-segment elevation meets Universal Definition of MI criteria.
Fibrinolysis is not helpful and potentially harmful without ST-elevation.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | LIMITATION OF INFARCT SIZE | TABLE 286-2 Electrocardiographic Criteria for ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | LIMITATION OF INFARCT SIZE | TABLE 286-2 Electrocardiographic Criteria for ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction
items.title: Electrocardiographic Criteria and Limitation of Infarct Size in STEMI
items.content: The electrocardiographic criteria for diagnosing STEMI involve specific ST-elevation measurements at the J-point. Timely reperfusion therapy can limit infarct size by improving perfusion and protecting ischemic myocardium.
keyPoints: ST-elevation cut-points: ≥0.2 mV (2 mm) in men ≥40 years, ≥0.25 mV (2.5 mm) in men <40 years, and ≥0.15 mV (1.5 mm) in women regardless of age.
Up to one-third of STEMI patients may achieve spontaneous reperfusion within 24 hours.
Reperfusion therapy accelerates artery opening and improves myocardial perfusion.
Avoid glucocorticoids and NSAIDs except aspirin due to potential harm.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | LIMITATION OF INFARCT SIZE | TABLE 286-2 Electrocardiographic Criteria for ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction

items.title: Why Primary PCI is Preferred Over Fibrinolysis for STEMI?
items.content: Primary PCI, typically involving stenting without preceding fibrinolysis, is more effective than fibrinolytic therapy in restoring perfusion during an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). It is particularly advantageous when performed within the first few hours of MI by experienced teams at dedicated centers. Primary PCI should be preferred over fibrinolysis unless the time to deliver the first coronary device exceeds 120 minutes.
keyPoints: Primary PCI restores perfusion more effectively than fibrinolytic therapy in STEMI.
Preferred when performed within the first few hours of MI by experienced teams.
Should be chosen over fibrinolysis unless time to device delivery is >120 min.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PRIMARY PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTION

items.title: When to Consider PCI on Nonculprit Arteries in STEMI?
items.content: In patients with STEMI without shock, performing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) on nonculprit arteries during the index procedure or within 45 days can reduce cardiovascular events and subsequent ischemia-driven revascularization. However, for patients presenting with cardiogenic shock, PCI of nonculprit arteries is contraindicated due to increased risk of death or severe renal failure.
keyPoints: PCI on nonculprit arteries reduces cardiovascular events in STEMI without shock.
Should be performed during the index procedure or within 45 days.
Contraindicated in cardiogenic shock due to higher mortality and renal failure risks.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PRIMARY PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTION

items.title: Timing and Indications for Fibrinolytic Therapy in STEMI
items.content: Fibrinolytic therapy should be initiated within 30 minutes of presentation if primary PCI is unavailable and no contraindications exist. The goal is to restore full coronary arterial patency.
keyPoints: Initiate fibrinolysis within door-to-needle time ≤ 30 min if primary PCI not available
Fibrinolytic agents include tPA, streptokinase, TNK, and rPA

Clear contraindications: history of cerebrovascular hemorrhage, recent nonhemorrhagic stroke or other cerebrovascular event within the past year, marked hypertension (systolic >180 mmHg or diastolic >110 mmHg), suspicion of aortic dissection, active internal bleeding

Relative contraindications include current use of anticoagulants (INR ≥ 2), recent invasive procedures (<2 weeks) or prolonged CPR (>10 min), known bleeding diathesis, pregnancy, hemorrhagic ophthalmic conditions, active peptic ulcer disease, history of severe hypertension currently controlled
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.title: Effectiveness and Administration of Fibrinolytic Agents
items.content: tPA, rPA, and TNK are more effective than streptokinase at achieving TIMI grade 3 coronary flow. Each agent has specific administration protocols.
keyPoints: tPA regimen: 15 mg bolus, followed by 50 mg over 30 min, then 35 mg over next 60 min
Streptokinase administered as 1.5 million units IV over 1 hour
rPA double-bolus regimen: 10-MU bolus over 2–3 min, second 10-MU bolus 30 min later
TNK given as single weight-based bolus of 0.53 mg/kg over 10 s
tPA, rPA, and TNK more effective than streptokinase at achieving TIMI grade 3 flow
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.title: Complications and Considerations in Fibrinolytic Therapy
items.content: Hemorrhage is the most serious complication of fibrinolytic therapy, with intracranial hemorrhage rates varying by agent and patient age.
keyPoints: Hemorrhagic stroke occurs in ~0.5–0.9% of patients treated with fibrinolytics
Intracranial hemorrhage rate is higher with tPA or rPA compared to streptokinase
Patients >70 years have roughly twice the intracranial hemorrhage rate as those <65 years
Allergic reactions to streptokinase occur in ~2% of patients, with marked hypotension being rare but serious
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.subtopic: INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS
items.title: Integrated Reperfusion Strategy for STEMI Management
items.content: The integrated reperfusion strategy emphasizes timely performance of PCI as the preferred method for managing STEMI. This approach involves a regional system that includes continuous quality improvement and coordination between emergency medical services and hospitals.
keyPoints: PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI.
An integrated system should include assessment and continuous quality improvement.
Urgent coronary angiography is recommended if there's failure of reperfusion or coronary artery reocclusion after fibrinolysis.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.subtopic: INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS
items.title: Post-Fibrinolytic Management in STEMI Patients
items.content: After fibrinolytic treatment, patients should be transferred to a PCI-capable center for potential angiography and PCI of the infarct-related artery within 3-24 hours if successful. Coronary artery bypass surgery is reserved for specific cases where PCI is unsuitable.
keyPoints: Transfer to a PCI-capable center post-fibrinolysis is recommended between 3-24 hours.
Coronary artery bypass surgery is considered when PCI is not suitable and revascularization is necessary.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.title: Management in Cardiac Intensive Care Units for STEMI Patients
items.content: Cardiac intensive care units provide continuous cardiac rhythm monitoring and hemodynamic support. High-risk patients with features like hemodynamic or electrical instability should be admitted to these units, while lower-risk patients can be managed in intermediate care units.
keyPoints: Continuous cardiac rhythm and selective hemodynamic monitoring are essential.
High-risk STEMI patients require admission to cardiac intensive care units.
Low-risk patients may be transferred out of the unit within 24-48 hours.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.title: Role and Benefits of Aspirin in STEMI Management
items.content: Aspirin is the standard antiplatelet agent for STEMI patients, significantly reducing mortality. The Antiplatelet Trialists’ Collaboration demonstrated a 27% relative reduction in mortality rate from 14.2% to 10.4% with aspirin use.
keyPoints: Aspirin reduces mortality by 27%, lowering the rate from 14.2% to 10.4%
Aspirin is standard antiplatelet therapy for STEMI
Evidence from Antiplatelet Trialists’ Collaboration
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ANTITHROMBOTIC AGENTS

items.title: Use of P2Y12 Inhibitors in STEMI Patients Undergoing PCI
items.content: P2Y12 inhibitors like clopidogrel, prasugrel, and ticagrelor reduce clinical events in STEMI patients. Third-generation agents (prasugrel, ticagrelor) are more effective than clopidogrel but increase bleeding risk.
keyPoints: Clopidogrel reduces reocclusion when combined with aspirin
Prasugrel and ticagrelor are more effective than clopidogrel in preventing ischemic complications during PCI
Increased bleeding risk associated with prasugrel and ticagrelor
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ANTITHROMBOTIC AGENTS

items.title: Comparative Efficacy of UFH, LMWH, and Bivalirudin in STEMI
items.content: UFH is commonly used with aspirin for primary PCI. Enoxaparin (LMWH) offers net clinical benefit over UFH despite higher bleeding rates. Bivalirudin reduces bleeding compared to UFH plus glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors.
keyPoints: UFH recommended in STEMI patients undergoing primary PCI
Enoxaparin reduces death/nonfatal reinfarction and urgent revascularization more than UFH
Bivalirudin associated with lower bleeding rates compared to UFH plus glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ANTITHROMBOTIC AGENTS

items.subtopic: BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM
items.title: Role of Beta Blockers in STEMI Management
items.content: Beta blockers provide acute and long-term benefits for patients with STEMI. Acutely, they improve the myocardial O2 supply-demand relationship, reduce pain, infarct size, and serious ventricular arrhythmias. Long-term oral therapy is beneficial unless contraindicated due to conditions like heart failure or severe LV dysfunction.
keyPoints: Acute intravenous beta blockade decreases infarct size and serious ventricular arrhythmias but is not routinely recommended due to the risk of precipitating heart failure.
Oral beta-blocker therapy post-STEMI reduces recurrent ischemia and reinfarction, except in specific contraindications (e.g., HF, severely compromised LV function).
Patients with an expected mortality rate <1% per year may not benefit significantly from long-term beta-blocker therapy.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM
items.title: ACE Inhibitors in STEMI Management
items.content: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality post-STEMI, with benefits additive to aspirin and beta blockers. They are beneficial for high-risk patients and all hemodynamically stable patients (systolic pressure >100 mmHg). ACE inhibitors should be continued indefinitely in cases of HF or reduced LV function.
keyPoints: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality post-STEMI, especially in high-risk patients with anterior infarction, prior infarction, or depressed LV function.
Short-term benefits are seen when prescribed to all hemodynamically stable STEMI patients (systolic pressure >100 mmHg).
Indefinite continuation of ACE inhibitors is recommended for clinically evident HF, reduced global LV function, large regional wall motion abnormality, or hypertension.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: OTHER AGENTS | COMPLICATIONS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT | VENTRICULAR DYSFFUNCTION
items.title: Use of Intravenous Nitroglycerin in STEMI
items.content: Intravenous nitroglycerin was once routinely used for the first 24–48 hours after STEMI onset to aid ischemic process and ventricular remodeling. However, recent large randomized trials have shown no benefit regarding major outcomes.
keyPoints: Initial dose: 5–10 μg/min
Maximum dose: up to 200 μg/min if hemodynamic stability is maintained
Consider use in patients with STEMI who have persistent hypertension, heart failure (HF), or ongoing ischemia
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER AGENTS | COMPLICATIONS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT | VENTRICULAR DYSFFUNCTION

items.subtopic: OTHER AGENTS | COMPLICATIONS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT | VENTRICULAR DYSFFUNCTION
items.title: Management of Ventricular Dysfunction Post-STEMI
items.content: Post-STEMI, ventricular remodeling involves changes in the left ventricle's shape, size, and thickness. This process can lead to heart failure (HF) over time. Therapy with ACE inhibitors is recommended to mitigate progressive dilation.
keyPoints: Ventricular remodeling occurs after STEMI
Greater dilation follows infarction of the anterior wall and apex
Prescribe ACE inhibitors or ARBs for ejection fraction <40%
Beta blockers should eventually be added
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER AGENTS | COMPLICATIONS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT | VENTRICULAR DYSFFUNCTION

items.title: Understanding Killip Classification for STEMI Patients
items.content: The Killip classification system categorizes patients with ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) based on the severity of heart failure symptoms, aiding in predicting mortality and guiding management strategies.
keyPoints: Class I: No signs of pulmonary or venous congestion; expected hospital mortality rate 0–5%.
Class II: Moderate HF with rales at lung bases, S3 gallop, tachypnea, right heart failure signs; expected mortality 10–20%.
Class III: Severe HF and pulmonary edema; expected mortality 35–45%.
Class IV: Shock with systolic pressure <90 mmHg, peripheral vasoconstriction, cyanosis, confusion, oliguria; expected mortality 85–95%.
Mortality rates have decreased by one-third to one-half due to advances in management, except for class IV where it has plateaued.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HEMODYNAMIC ASSESSMENT

items.title: Hemodynamic Monitoring and Management in STEMI
items.content: Monitoring left ventricular (LV) function using Swan-Ganz catheterization is crucial for managing hemodynamics in STEMI patients, particularly those with hypotension or heart failure symptoms.
keyPoints: Abnormal global LV function occurs when 20–25% of the left ventricle's contraction is impaired.
Infarction of ≥40% of the left ventricle often leads to cardiogenic shock.
Swan-Ganz catheter placement allows monitoring of LV filling pressure and estimation of cardiac output.
Intraarterial pressure monitoring helps calculate systemic vascular resistance for vasopressor/vasodilator therapy adjustment.
Patients with elevated LV filling pressures (>22 mmHg) and normal cardiac indices (2.6–3.6 L/[min/m²]) benefit from diuresis.
Patients with low LV filling pressures (<15 mmHg) and reduced cardiac indices may respond to volume expansion.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HEMODYNAMIC ASSESSMENT

items.title: Management of Hypovolemia in STEMI Patients with Hypotension
items.content: Hypovolemia can contribute to hypotension and vascular collapse in some STEMI patients. It may result from previous diuretic use, reduced fluid intake, or vomiting due to pain or medications. Identifying and correcting hypovolemia through cautious fluid administration during continuous oxygenation monitoring is crucial before initiating more aggressive therapies.
keyPoints: Hypovolemia can be an easily corrected condition contributing to hypotension in STEMI patients.
Causes include previous diuretic use, reduced fluid intake, and vomiting associated with pain or medications.
Corrective measures involve cautious fluid administration under continuous oxygenation monitoring.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HYPOVOLEMIA | Heart Failure | CARDIOGENIC SHOCK

items.title: Cardiogenic Shock Incidence and Management in STEMI Patients
items.content: The incidence of cardiogenic shock has decreased from 20% to approximately 7% due to prompt reperfusion, efforts to reduce infarct size, and treatment of ongoing ischemia. Among those who develop cardiogenic shock, 90% do so during hospitalization, often with severe multivessel coronary artery disease.
keyPoints: Incidence of cardiogenic shock has reduced from 20% to ~7%.
90% of patients with cardiogenic shock develop it during hospitalization.
Patients typically have severe multivessel coronary artery disease and piecemeal necrosis.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HYPOVOLEMIA | Heart Failure | CARDIOGENIC SHOCK

items.title: Identifying Right Ventricular Infarction: ECG and Hemodynamics
items.content: Right ventricular (RV) infarction is often associated with inferior myocardial infarctions. Clinically significant RV infarction can be identified by specific signs of right heart failure, such as jugular venous distention, Kussmaul's sign, and hepatomegaly. ECG findings in the first 24 hours typically include ST-segment elevations in right-sided precordial leads, especially lead V4R.
keyPoints: Approximately one-third of patients with inferior infarction show RV necrosis.
Signs of severe RV failure: jugular venous distention, Kussmaul's sign, hepatomegaly.
ECG: ST-segment elevations in right-sided leads, particularly V4R, within the first 24 hours.
Hemodynamic pattern on catheterization resembles constrictive pericarditis (steep right atrial 'y' descent and early diastolic dip and plateau in RV waveforms).
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · RIGHT VENTRICULAR INFARCTION

items.title: Management of Right Ventricular Infarction: Volume Expansion and Hemodynamic Support
items.content: The management of right ventricular infarction focuses on maintaining adequate RV preload through volume expansion. This approach helps improve left ventricular performance, which in turn reduces pulmonary capillary wedge pressure and pulmonary arterial pressures.
keyPoints: Therapy involves volume expansion to maintain adequate RV preload.
Aim is to improve LV performance, reducing pulmonary capillary wedge and pulmonary arterial pressures.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · RIGHT VENTRICULAR INFARCTION

items.title: Management of Arrhythmias in STEMI
items.content: Arrhythmias are common after STEMI, especially early on. They result from autonomic imbalance, electrolyte disturbances, and ischemia. Prompt treatment is crucial as most arrhythmia-related deaths occur within the first few hours post-infarction.
keyPoints: Prompt management of arrhythmias reduces mortality in STEMI patients.
Most deaths from arrhythmia occur during the first few hours after infarction.
Effective treatment requires trained personnel and appropriate equipment.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment of Ventricular Premature Beats in STEMI
items.content: Infrequent ventricular premature beats are common post-STEMI but usually do not require therapy. Prophylactic antiarrhythmic drugs should be avoided unless there are sustained ventricular arrhythmias, as they can increase mortality.
keyPoints: Ventricular premature beats often occur in STEMI and typically don't need treatment.
Prophylactic antiarrhythmic therapy is contraindicated without clinically significant ventricular tachyarrhythmias.
Beta-blockers are recommended to manage ventricular ectopic activity unless contraindicated.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia and Fibrillation in STEMI
items.content: Sustained ventricular tachycardia should be treated with intravenous amiodarone or procainamide. For hemodynamically unstable cases, immediate electrical cardioversion is necessary. Long-term prognosis varies based on the timing of arrhythmia onset.
keyPoints: Amiodarone regimen: 150 mg bolus over 10 min, then 1.0 mg/min for 6 h, followed by 0.5 mg/min.
Procainamide alternative: 15 mg/kg bolus over 20–30 min; infusion of 1–4 mg/min.
Unsynchronized discharge of 200 J is used for ventricular fibrillation or unstable tachycardia.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 1)

items.title: Management of Sinus Tachycardia in ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Sinus tachycardia is the most common supraventricular arrhythmia and should be managed by addressing its underlying cause. If due to sympathetic overstimulation, beta blockers are indicated.
keyPoints: Most common supraventricular arrhythmia
Treat primary problem if secondary (e.g., anemia, fever, HF)
Beta blocker treatment for sympathetic overstimulation
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 2)

items.title: Indications and Management of Atrioventricular Block in Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Complete AV block with anterior infarction has higher mortality than with inferior infarction due to ischemic conduction system damage. Temporary pacing is beneficial for inferoposterior infarction with complete heart block.
keyPoints: Higher in-hospital and post-discharge mortality with anterior infarction
AV block in inferior infarction often transient
Temporary pacing recommended for inferoposterior infarction with HF, hypotension, or significant bradycardia
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Sinus Bradycardia in Acute Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Sinus bradycardia should be treated if it causes hemodynamic compromise. Atropine is the first-line treatment, with electrical pacing considered for persistent bradycardia.
keyPoints: Treat if heart rate <50–60 beats/min causing hemodynamic issues
Atropine: 1.0 mg IV initially, up to 3.0 mg total
Electrical pacing for persistent bradycardia (<40 beats/min)
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 2)

items.title: Recurrent Chest Discomfort in STEMI: Prompt Intervention Needed
items.content: Recurrent or persistent ischemia after an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) often indicates extension of the original infarct or a new reinfarction, significantly increasing mortality risk. Patients presenting with these symptoms should be referred for immediate coronary arteriography and mechanical revascularization to mitigate risks.
keyPoints: Recurrent ischemia can herald infarct extension or reinfarction.
Mortality nearly triples after STEMI if recurrent ischemia occurs.
Prompt referral for coronary arteriography and mechanical revascularization is recommended.
Fibrinolytic agents may be used as an alternative to early mechanical revascularization.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Pericarditis Post-STEMI: Accurate Diagnosis Crucial
items.content: Post-STEMI pericarditis, characterized by friction rubs and/or pain, can be mistaken for recurrent ischemia. Aspirin is the treatment of choice; however, misdiagnosis may lead to inappropriate treatments like anticoagulants or coronary arteriography. Pain radiating to trapezius muscles suggests pericarditis rather than ischemic discomfort.
keyPoints: Pericarditis often follows STEMI involving the epicardium.
Aspirin (650 mg four times daily) is used for management.
Misdiagnosis can lead to inappropriate use of anticoagulants or coronary arteriography.
Pain radiating to trapezius muscles indicates pericarditis.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Thromboembolism in STEMI: Recognize and Treat
items.content: Thromboembolism is a significant complication of STEMI, often clinically silent but detectable at necropsy. It occurs more frequently with large infarcts, heart failure, or an LV thrombus. Echocardiography can identify LV thrombi, guiding the need for systemic anticoagulation to reduce embolic complications.
keyPoints: Thromboembolism complicates ~10% of STEMI cases clinically but is found in 20% at necropsy.
It contributes to death in 25% of hospitalized STEMI patients who die.
Occurs with large infarcts, heart failure, or LV thrombus detected by echocardiography.
Systemic anticoagulation reduces embolic complications; duration is typically 3–6 months.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Identifying High-Risk Factors Post-STEMI
items.content: After initial recovery from STEMI, certain clinical and laboratory factors increase cardiovascular risk. Persistent ischemia, depressed LV ejection fraction (<40%), rales or congestion on imaging, and symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias are critical indicators.
keyPoints: Persistent ischemia (spontaneous or provoked)
Depressed LV ejection fraction (<40%)
Rales above lung bases or congestion on chest imaging
Symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · POSTINFARCTION RISK STRATIFICATION AND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Strategies for Evaluating and Managing Post-STEMI Risk
items.content: Post-infarction strategies include risk evaluation through exercise stress testing, cardiac catheterization with coronary angiography, or invasive electrophysiologic evaluation. High-risk patients are identified by low workload-induced angina, large reversible defects on perfusion imaging, ischemia, and symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias during exercise.
keyPoints: Submaximal exercise stress testing before discharge
Maximal (symptom-limited) exercise test 4–6 weeks post-infarction
High-risk indicators: low workload-induced angina, large reversible perfusion defects, ischemia, symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · POSTINFARCTION RISK STRATIFICATION AND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Role of Imaging in Post-STEMI Management
items.content: Evaluation of LV function is crucial post-STEMI. Echocardiography, cardiac MRI, or nuclear myocardial perfusion imaging identify patients needing renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors. Cardiac MRI with gadolinium highlights infarct zones by contrasting bright signals in damaged areas against dark normal myocardium.
keyPoints: Evaluation of LV function using echocardiography, cardiac MRI, or nuclear imaging
Use of renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors for depressed LV ejection fraction
Cardiac MRI with gadolinium highlights infarct zones
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · POSTINFARCTION RISK STRATIFICATION AND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Role of Antiplatelet Therapy in STEMI Secondary Prevention
items.content: Long-term antiplatelet therapy significantly reduces the risk of recurrent cardiovascular events after a STEMI. Aspirin alone offers substantial benefits, while dual antiplatelet therapy is recommended for 12 months unless contraindicated by bleeding risks.
keyPoints: Aspirin use post-STEMI results in a 25% reduction in recurrent infarction, stroke, or cardiovascular mortality (36 fewer events per 1000 patients treated).
Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and a P2Y12 receptor antagonist is recommended for 12 months after STEMI.
Considerations include the patient's bleeding risk.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.title: Importance of ACE Inhibitors/ARBs in Post-STEMI Management
items.content: ACE inhibitors or ARBs, along with aldosterone antagonists for selected patients, are crucial for preventing late ventricular remodeling and recurrent ischemic events after STEMI.
keyPoints: Indicated indefinitely for patients with clinically evident heart failure, reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, or large regional wall motion abnormalities.
Preventive role in late ventricular remodeling and recurrence of ischemic events.

Primary PCI has lower short-term and long-term clinical outcomes compared to fibrinolysis.
Primary PCI is less effective than fibrinolysis in opening occluded coronary arteries.
Primary PCI should only be used when the time to delivery of the first coronary device is longer than 120 minutes.
items.explanation: Primary PCI can be performed on patients with contraindications to fibrinolytic therapy.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PRIMARY PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTION

items.question: When is primary PCI preferred over fibrinolysis in STEMI management?
options: When the time to delivery of the first coronary device is anticipated to be less than 120 minutes.
When the diagnosis is certain and there are no contraindications for fibrinolytic therapy.
When symptoms have been present for longer than 2–3 hours, making the clot harder to lyse with fibrinolytic drugs.
When performed by inexperienced teams in non-dedicated medical centers.
items.explanation: Primary PCI is preferred when symptoms have been present for longer than 2-3 hours as clots are less easily lysed by fibrinolytic drugs at this time.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PRIMARY PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended approach to PCI in STEMI patients with cardiogenic shock?
options: Routine PCI of nonculprit arteries during the initial primary PCI should be performed.
PCI on nonculprit coronary vessels within 45 days is preferred.
Culprit-only PCI is recommended due to increased risk of death or severe renal failure with routine PCI of nonculprit arteries.
Primary PCI should not be considered in patients with cardiogenic shock.
items.explanation: In STEMI patients with cardiogenic shock, culprit-only PCI is recommended as routine PCI of nonculprit arteries increases the risk of death or severe renal failure.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · PRIMARY PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended initial dose regimen for tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) in STEMI patients?
options: A 15-mg bolus followed by 50 mg over 30 min, then 35 mg over 60 min.
A double-bolus regimen of 10-MU each given 30 minutes apart.
A single weight-based bolus of 0.53 mg/kg over 10 seconds.
items.explanation: The recommended regimen for tPA consists of a 15-mg bolus followed by 50 mg intravenously over the first 30 min, then 35 mg over the next 60 min.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.question: Which fibrinolytic agent is administered as a single weight-based intravenous bolus?
items.explanation: TNK is given as a single weight-based intravenous bolus of 0.53 mg/kg over 10 seconds.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.question: What TIMI grade indicates full perfusion with normal flow in the infarct-related coronary artery?
items.explanation: TIMI grade 3 indicates full perfusion of the infarct vessel with normal flow.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.question: Which fibrinolytic agent is associated with a higher rate of intracranial hemorrhage compared to streptokinase?
items.explanation: Large-scale trials have suggested that the rate of intracranial hemorrhage with tPA or rPA is slightly higher than with streptokinase.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · FIBRINOLYSIS

items.question: What is the recommended action if a patient treated with fibrinolytic therapy shows persistent chest pain and ST-segment elevation >90 minutes?
options: Perform urgent coronary angiography and consider rescue PCI.
Transfer to an intermediate care unit immediately.
items.explanation: Urgent coronary angiography should be performed if there is evidence of failure of reperfusion, and rescue PCI should be considered.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.question: When is transfer to a PCI-capable center recommended after fibrinolysis?
options: Immediately after successful fibrinolysis with intent for angiography and PCI between 3 and 24 hours.
Only if the patient develops recurrent ischemia within 6 hours.
After coronary artery bypass surgery is deemed necessary.
Within the first hour post-fibrinolysis regardless of symptoms.
items.explanation: Transfer to a PCI-capable center is recommended immediately after fibrinolysis with intent for angiography and PCI between 3 and 24 hours if indicated.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.question: What type of unit should patients with STEMI be admitted to when high-risk features are present?
items.explanation: Patients with STEMI should be admitted to a cardiac intensive care unit when high-risk features are present.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.question: What is the recommended dietary fat content for patients after an acute myocardial infarction?
items.explanation: The typical diet after AMI should provide ≤30% of total calories as fat.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · INTEGRATED REPERFUSION STRATEGY | CARDIAC INTENSIVE CARE UNITS

items.question: Which antiplatelet agent is the standard treatment for patients with STEMI?
items.explanation: Aspirin is the standard antiplatelet agent for patients with STEMI.

items.question: What relative reduction in mortality rate was observed with antiplatelet therapy in MI patients according to the Antiplatelet Trialists’ Collaboration?
items.explanation: The pooled data revealed a relative reduction of 27% in the mortality rate.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ANTITHROMBOTIC AGENTS

items.question: Which P2Y12 inhibitor is associated with an increased risk of bleeding compared to clopidogrel?
items.explanation: Third-generation oral P2Y12 ADP receptor antagonists, such as prasugrel and ticagrelor, are associated with an increased risk of bleeding compared to clopidogrel.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ANTITHROMBOTIC AGENTS

items.question: Which anticoagulant is most commonly used in clinical practice for STEMI patients undergoing primary PCI?
items.explanation: The anticoagulant agent most commonly used in clinical practice is unfractionated heparin (UFH).
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ANTITHROMBOTIC AGENTS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of acute intravenous beta blockade in patients with STEMI?
options: Decreases incidence of serious ventricular arrhythmias
items.explanation: Acute intravenous beta blockade decreases, not increases, the incidence of serious ventricular arrhythmias and reduces infarct size.

items.question: In which patients is oral beta-blocker therapy after STEMI generally contraindicated?
options: Patients with heart failure or severely compromised LV function
All patients treated with an ACE inhibitor
Patients without a history of asthma
items.explanation: Oral beta-blocker therapy is contraindicated in patients with heart failure or severely compromised LV function.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM

items.subtopic: BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM
items.question: Which statement is true regarding ACE inhibitors in STEMI patients?
options: ACE inhibitors are only beneficial for high-risk patients.
The mortality benefits of ACE inhibitors are not additive to those achieved with aspirin and beta blockers.
Short-term benefit occurs when prescribed unselectively to all hemodynamically stable STEMI patients.

items.explanation: ACE inhibitors provide a short-term benefit even when prescribed unselectively to all hemodynamically stable STEMI patients.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · BETA-ADRENOCEPTOR BLOCKERS | INHIBITION OF THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM

items.question: Which agent is recommended for routine use in patients with STEMI who have persistent hypertension, HF, or ongoing ischemia?
items.explanation: Intravenous nitroglycerin may be considered for patients with STEMI who have persistent hypertension, HF, or ongoing ischemia.

items.question: What is the role of calcium antagonists in the treatment of most patients with STEMI?
options: They are recommended for routine use.
Their use has been established by multiple trials.
There is no established role for these agents.
They should be used to control blood glucose.
items.explanation: Results of multiple trials have failed to establish a role for calcium antagonists in the treatment of most patients with STEMI.

items.question: What should be done if serum magnesium deficits are demonstrated on admission to minimize the risk of arrhythmias?

items.question: Which clinical signs are most commonly associated with pump failure in STEMI patients?

items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HEMODYNAMIC ASSESSMENT

items.question: According to Killip's classification, what is the expected hospital mortality rate for class IV patients?
items.explanation: Class IV patients have an expected hospital mortality rate of 85–95%.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HEMODYNAMIC ASSESSMENT

items.question: What hemodynamic finding is characteristic in STEMI but may result from either diastolic or systolic failure?
options: Elevated LV filling pressure and elevated pulmonary artery pressure
items.explanation: Elevated LV filling pressure and elevated pulmonary artery pressure are characteristic findings.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HEMODYNAMIC ASSESSMENT

items.question: Which condition is considered easily corrected and may contribute to hypotension during STEMI?
items.explanation: Hypovolemia can be easily corrected by cautious fluid administration.

items.question: Which medication is noted for its effectiveness in reducing pulmonary congestion during systolic and/or diastolic heart failure associated with STEMI?
items.explanation: Diuretic agents like furosemide are extremely effective in diminishing pulmonary congestion.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HYPOVOLEMIA | Heart Failure | CARDIOGENIC SHOCK

items.question: What is the primary benefit of using nitrates over diuretics during STEMI management?
options: They lower preload through venodilation without decreasing plasma volume
They improve ventricular compliance by reducing LV filling pressure
They are more effective than ACE inhibitors
items.explanation: Nitrates lower preload through venodilation without decreasing the total plasma volume.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · HYPOVOLEMIA | Heart Failure | CARDIOGENIC SHOCK

items.question: Which ECG lead is most frequently associated with ST-segment elevations in patients with right ventricular infarction?
items.explanation: ST-segment elevations of right-sided precordial ECG leads, particularly lead V4R, are frequently present in the first 24 hours.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · RIGHT VENTRICULAR INFARCTION

items.question: What clinical signs indicate severe right ventricular failure?
options: Jugular venous distention, Kussmaul's sign, hepatomegaly
items.explanation: Clinically significant RV infarction causes signs of severe RV failure including jugular venous distention, Kussmaul's sign, hepatomegaly.

items.question: What hemodynamic pattern is often revealed by catheterization in right ventricular infarction?
options: Steep right atrial 'y' descent and an early diastolic dip and plateau in RV waveforms
items.explanation: Catheterization often reveals a distinctive hemodynamic pattern resembling constrictive pericarditis, including steep right atrial 'y' descent.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · RIGHT VENTRICULAR INFARCTION

items.question: Which of the following agents are contraindicated for prophylactic antiarrhythmic therapy in patients with ventricular premature beats without clinically important ventricular tachyarrhythmias?

items.question: Which electrolyte disturbances are risk factors for ventricular fibrillation in STEMI patients?

items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended energy level for unsynchronized discharge during electrical cardioversion of ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation?
items.explanation: An unsynchronized discharge of 200 J is used immediately in patients with ventricular fibrillation or when ventricular tachycardia causes hemodynamic deterioration.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 1)

items.question: Which medication is indicated for sinus tachycardia due to sympathetic overstimulation?
items.explanation: Treatment with a beta blocker is indicated for sinus tachycardia due to sympathetic overstimulation.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 2)

items.question: What is the initial intravenous dose of atropine recommended for treating hemodynamically significant sinus bradycardia?
items.explanation: Atropine is given intravenously in doses of 1.0 mg initially for sinus bradycardia.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · ARRHYTHMIAS (part 2)

items.question: In which type of myocardial infarction is complete atrioventricular block associated with higher mortality rates?

items.question: What pacing mode should be used for patients with sinus bradycardia unresponsive to drug therapy?

items.question: Which of the following is an appropriate management strategy for pericarditis in patients with STEMI?
options: Administration of anticoagulants to prevent tamponade.
Use of aspirin (650 mg four times daily).
Beta blockers to manage chest pain.
items.explanation: Aspirin is recommended for managing pericarditis in STEMI patients, while anticoagulants should be avoided due to the risk of tamponade.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is a key diagnostic feature that helps differentiate pericarditis from ischemic discomfort?
options: Pain radiating to the trapezius muscle.
Presence of an LV thrombus on echocardiography.
Recurrent chest discomfort with ST-segment elevation.
Use of nitrates for pain relief.
items.explanation: Pain radiating to the trapezius muscle is typical of pericarditis and helps differentiate it from ischemic discomfort.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.question: In which patients should systemic anticoagulation be considered following STEMI?
options: Patients with recurrent ischemic ST-segment elevation.
Patients with a clearly demonstrated thrombus by echocardiography.
Patients with pericarditis and persistent rub.
All patients admitted for STEMI as a preventive measure.
items.explanation: Systemic anticoagulation is recommended when a thrombus has been clearly demonstrated, to reduce the risk of embolic complications.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What complication can occur weeks to months after STEMI and involves dyskinesis or paradoxical wall motion?
items.explanation: Left Ventricular Aneurysm involves dyskinesis or paradoxical wall motion and can occur weeks to months after STEMI.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · OTHER COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which factor is NOT associated with increased cardiovascular risk after recovery from STEMI?
items.explanation: An LV ejection fraction >40% is not associated with increased risk; a depressed LV ejection fraction (<40%) is.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · POSTINFARCTION RISK STRATIFICATION AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: What imaging technique can identify myocardial infarction by showing bright signals in areas of infarction?
items.explanation: Cardiac MRI with gadolinium shows bright signals in areas of infarction due to the contrast agent entering the intercellular region.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · POSTINFARCTION RISK STRATIFICATION AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: When should a maximal exercise stress test be carried out after STEMI?

items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · POSTINFARCTION RISK STRATIFICATION AND MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which patients should be considered at high risk for recurrent MI or death from arrhythmia?

Those without ischemia during exercise testing
Those with no symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias
items.explanation: Patients with a large reversible defect on perfusion imaging, demonstrable ischemia, or provoked symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias are at high risk.

items.question: Which combination of antiplatelet therapy is recommended for 12 months after STEMI in patients without high bleeding risk?

items.explanation: Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and a P2Y12 receptor antagonist is recommended for 12 months after STEMI.

items.question: What is the long-term benefit of using ACE inhibitors or ARBs in patients with clinically evident HF, reduced LV ejection fraction, or large regional wall motion abnormality?

items.explanation: ACE inhibitors or ARBs prevent late ventricular remodeling and recurrent ischemic events.
items.reference: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction · SECONDARY PREVENTION

items.question: What is the role of low-dose colchicine in secondary prevention for STEMI patients?

It serves as a primary preventive measure.
